text.fullText: Comment to the Environmental Protection Agency Proposed Rule: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Benjamin Zycher* Senior Fellow, American Enterprise Institute February 17, 2026 RIN 2040-AG47 40 CFR Part 121, Document ID EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 FRL-6976.2-01-OW Federal Register Vol. 91, No. 10, Thursday January 15, 2026, pp. 2008-2042 Submitted through the federalregister.gov portal at https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/01/15/2026-00754/updating- the-water-quality-certification-regulations#open-comment This comment paper addresses the regulatory policy issues attendant upon the EPA proposal to revise the 2023 rule establishing processes for implementation of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act.1 At the most general level, the proposed rule represents a vast improvement over the 2023 final rule, in particular because the 2023 final rule facilitates massive obstruction of important infrastructure projects with little or no protection of water quality as defined in the CWA. Indeed, such obstructionism based upon section 401 often has been justified on grounds unrelated to water quality. At the same time, some improvements to the proposed rule are advisable. This comment is organized as follows. Summary. I. Examples of Projects Blocked or Delayed Using Improper Applications of Section 401. II. Reforms In the Proposed Rule That Should Be Finalized. III. Ways to Strengthen the Proposed Rule. IV. Conclusions. * Ph.D., Economics, University of California, Los Angeles. Senior fellow, American Enterprise Institute. The views expressed are solely those of the author. I can be reached at benjamin.zycher@aei.org. 1 The 2026 proposed rule is at https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/FR-2026-01-15/pdf/2026-00754.pdf. For the 2023 EPA final rule, see Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule, 88 FR 66558 (September 27, 2023). The American Enterprise Institute (AEI) is a nonpartisan, nonprofit, 501(c)(3) educational organization and does not take institutional positions on any issues. The views expressed in this testimony are those of the author. 2 Summary Section 401 of the Clean Water Act, as implemented in the 2023 final rule, allows for lengthy obstructionism of important infrastructure projects on grounds that are perverse economically and environmentally. Several examples are discussed below. The proposed rule contains several provisions that should be finalized: • Limiting the Scope of the CWA Section 401 to Actual Discharges. • Reforming Timeline Withdrawal and Resubmission Delays. • Standardizing Request Parameters. • Requiring Joint Agreement to Certification Modifications. • Establishing A Firm Hearing and Determinization Timeline for Neighboring-State Objections. Two areas of recommended strengthening are as follows: • Strengthen the Withdrawal/Resubmission Prohibition. • Specifying Categories for Neighboring State “May Affect” Determinations. I. Examples of Projects Blocked or Delayed Using Improper Applications of Section 401 It is no secret that obstruction of new energy infrastructure projects is a massive undertaking by ideological opponents of conventional (fossil, hydropower, and other) energy, by not-in-my-backyard interests, and by rent-seeking opponents of projects that would strengthen competitive conditions in specific markets.2 Section 401 of the CWA has been used repeatedly for that purpose, with arguments having little or nothing to do with water quality. A few examples are as follows. • The New York State Department of Environmental Conservation in 2016 denied approval for the Constitution Pipeline, which would have transported natural gas from the Marcellus basin in Pennsylvania to markets in New York via the Iroquois Gas Transmission and to New England via the Tennessee Gas Pipeline. NYSDEC cited climate change concerns and downstream greenhouse gas emissions from utilization of the natural gas supplies. With respect to water quality — after all, the central focus of the CWA — NYSDEC denied the application for a water quality certification on the grounds that Constitution had failed to provide information on stream crossings, and made reference to the purported "resistance" by Constitution in terms of responding adequately to NYSDEC's requests. Nowhere was there any determination in the denial of adverse effects on water quality.3 • NYSDEC in 2017 denied approval for the Millennium Valley Lateral pipeline, on 2 On reform of the implementing regulations for the National Environmental Policy Act, see Benjamin Zycher at https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Zycher_CEQ_NEPA_Comment_Nov_2021.pdf. 3 See https://uk.practicallaw.thomsonreuters.com/w-012- 8378?transitionType=Default&contextData=(sc.Default)&firstPage=true. New York now has opposed Constitution’s proposal to FERC to reissue its permit for the pipeline. See https://www.reuters.com/legal/litigation/ny-regulators-oppose-filings-proposed-constitution-pipeline-project-2026- 01-28/., 3 endangered species grounds and a purported inadequate FERC review with respect to greenhouse gas emissions.4 Those rationales have little to do with water quality. FERC later that year overturned the NYSDEC denial, on the grounds that NYSDEC had waived its certification authority by failing to act within the one-year statutory deadline, and was upheld by the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit.5 • The Washington State Department of Ecology in 2017 denied a water quality permit sought by Millennium Bulk Terminals to construct and operate a coal export terminal in Longview, WA, on the grounds that the terminal would have caused “significant and unavoidable harm” to nine environmental areas: air quality, vehicle traffic, vessel traffic, rail capacity, rail safety, [train] noise pollution, social and community resources, cultural resources, and tribal resources. The project parameters related to water quality were trivial: 24 acres of wetlands, 41.5 acres of dredging in the Columbia riverbed, and 537 pilings in the Columbia river. • The New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection in 2019 denied the PennEast Pipeline permit application incomplete, in a real-life “Catch-22” scenario in which the state denied PennEast’s eminent domain application for state-owned land, thus denying PennEast access to land for which the state required surveys.6 Despite winning at the Supreme Court on the eminent domain issue, PennEast cancelled the project in 2021, citing permitting difficulties.7 II. Reforms In the Proposed Rule That Should Be Finalized Limiting the Scope of the CWA Section 401 to Actual Discharges. The 2023 final rule implemented an “activity as a whole” definition for regulatory oversight of the certification process. This is inconsistent with the law, and the proposed rule returns the focus of certification requirements to actual point source discharges into the waters of the United States. Section 401(a)(1) requires certification for projects “which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters.” Certification must ensure “that any such discharge will comply with the applicable provisions of” the enumerated CWA sections.8 This shift from “activity as a whole” to “discharge” is consistent with the 1972 statutory change from “activity” to “discharge.” Reforming Timeline Withdrawal and Resubmission Delays. Ideological and special interests and agencies intent upon obstructing given projects often have circumvented the statutory 4 See Weiler and Stanford at https://www.eba-net.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/21-503-540-Weiler_FINAL.pdf. 5 See, respectively, https://anzlaw.thomsonreuters.com/w-010- 3851?originationContext=document&transitionType=DocumentItem&contextData=(sc.Default)&ppcid=15a87f064 3334f16b6bc87f02cde528f&firstPage=true and https://www.troutmanenergyreport.com/2018/03/second-circuit- affirms-ferc-ruling-ny-dec-waived-authority-cwa-permit-pipeline-project/. 6 See https://aglaw.psu.edu/shale-law-in-the-spotlight/penn-east-pipeline-project-u-s-court-of-appeals-denies- eminent-domain-proceedings-by-penneast-pipeline-company-llc-for-the-acquisition-of-new-jersey-state-owned- properties/. 7 See https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/20pdf/19-1039_8n5a.pdf and paragraph 9 at https://www.ferc.gov/sites/default/files/2020-06/C-2- 022020.pdf#:~:text=24%20The%20record%20demonstrates%20that,to%20commence%20and%20complete%20con struction. 8 See 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). 4 one-year deadline by advising applicants to withdraw and resubmit proposals, thus restarting the one-year clock, sometimes repeatedly. The proposed rule prohibits certifying authorities from circumventing the deadline through the withdrawal-and-resubmission process. It adds that “the certifying authority may not request the applicant to withdraw a request for certification or take any action to extend the reasonable period of time” beyond the explicit “shall not exceed one year” language of Section 401(a)(1).9 The obvious Congressional intent was to prevent agencies under Section 401 from ending project proposals by delaying them indefinitely. Standardizing Request Parameters. The proposed rule proscribes agencies from adding required parameters for certification applications, thus returning the process to a single federal standard for a complete application. This provision would be consistent with the Second Circuit decision in NYSDEC that the “plain language of Section 401 outlines a bright-line rule regarding the beginning of review.”10 Allowing agencies to decide when applications are “complete” would enable them to delay even the consideration of project proposals indefinitely. Requiring Joint Agreement to Certification Modifications. The proposed rule requires that the relevant federal agency, the certifying agency, and the applicant agree to modifications of certifications already issued before any modifications are implemented, and the applicant must agree to the language of the modification. This provision of the proposed rule avoids an obvious bait-and-switch problem affecting not only given projects but the project investment process writ large. It also avoids the problem of agencies using the modification process to extent the review deadlines beyond the one-year statutory limit. Establishing A Firm Hearing and Determinization Timeline for Neighboring-State Objections. Section 401(a)(2) establishes specific timeframes for each part of the neighboring- state objection process, except for the public hearing component. States notified that a certification may affect their water quality requirements have sixty days to determine whether a discharge “will violate” those requirements, but the CWA does not specify the timing limits on the public hearing process and the final ruling by the federal agency. The proposed rule establishes a 90-day time limit for the public hearing and final determination process. This is necessary to avoid unreasonable delays. III. Ways to Strengthen the Proposed Rule Strengthen Withdrawal/Resubmission Prohibition. EPA asks for comments on whether the proposed text “sufficiently addresses the regulatory uncertainty surrounding withdrawal and resubmission.” Additional specificity would be useful. The final rule should make it clear that the one-year timeline begins with the initial receipt of a complete application, regardless of any subsequent withdrawal initiated or requested by the agency. Specifying Categories for Neighboring State “May Affect” Determinations. EPA asks for comments on categories for “may affect” determinations with respect to other states’ water quality. Exclusions should include discharges into waters with no hydrologic connection to other states, small and/or temporary discharges that cannot flow to other states, and discharges in isolated coastal areas with no bodies of water shared with other states. 9 Ibid. 10 See 884 F.3d at 455–56. 5 IV. Conclusions This proposed rule imposes important limits on CWA Section 401 agency obstruction of important infrastructure projects, driven by opposition ideological, political, and economic. These limits should be finalized. There are a few areas in which the proposed rule could be strengthened, which the final rule should reflect.
